mnemonics: STRUCTURE: Stores Several Types Under One Roof Efficiently. UNION: Uses One Name, Interchanges Occupants Now.
keyPoints: A structure (struct) groups different data types together, with each member having its own memory space.
A union groups different data types together, but all members share the same memory location.
Only one member of a union can contain a value at any given time, as writing to one member overwrites the others.
commonMistakes: Treating a union's members as if they all contain valid data simultaneously, leading to incorrect values.
Forgetting that the size of a struct is at least the sum of its members, while a union's size is that of its largest member.
Using a union without a 'tag' or external variable to track which member is currently being stored (especially in non-discriminated unions).
